preamble: The shift from the voiced palatal nasal [ɲ] to the palatal glide [j] (y) is a process of reduction or lenition. In this change, the tongue loses its full stop contact with the hard palate, often resulting in a nasalized glide that may eventually lose its nasal quality entirely.

examples.note: Widespread Brazilian realization where the palatal nasal /ɲ/ (nh) is reduced to a nasalized palatal glide.
Systematic reduction of the historical French palatal nasal into a nasalized glide between vowels.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
